params.text:
Involving the person and their family

A care plan belongs to the person it is about. The Care Act 2014 requires the person to be involved in preparing their own plan, and to have a genuine say in how their needs are met. A plan written without the person is unlikely to reflect what truly matters to them.

  • The person first: their views, wishes and feelings lead the plan.
  • Family and friends: those close to the person can add valuable knowledge, with the person's agreement.
  • Advocacy: where someone would struggle to be involved, an independent advocate can help their voice be heard.

Involvement is not a one-off signature; it continues every time the plan is delivered and reviewed.

Family knowledge and the person's voice

Family members and friends often hold years of knowledge about a person — their routines, their likes and dislikes, what settles them when they are upset. With the person's consent, this information makes a plan richer and more accurate. It is especially valuable when someone finds it hard to express their own wishes.

Even so, the person's own voice always comes first. Family views should support, not override, what the individual wants, and you should never assume a relative speaks for the person unless they have a legal right to do so. In your daily work you help keep people involved: you check preferences rather than assuming them, and you pass on anything new so the plan reflects the person as they are now.

params.question: A person should be actively involved in preparing and reviewing their own care and support plan.

l10n.wrongAnswerMessage: Not quite. Under the Care Act 2014 the person must be involved in their own plan; it is not written for them alone.
l10n.correctAnswerMessage: Correct. The Care Act 2014 requires the person to be involved, so the plan reflects what matters to them.
